steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim has two specific parts: (1) the speaker addresses those who mistreat people with less privilege, and (2) the speaker says those people will receive divine judgment as a consequence. The correct quotation must contain both the mistreatment and the divine punishment.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "Men may tread down the poor and lowly, / May crush them in anger and hate, / But surely the mills of God's justice / Will grind out the grist of their fate." (from "An Appeal to My Countrywomen")": The first two lines name the mistreatment (treading down the poor, crushing them in anger), and the last two lines deliver divine judgment as the result. Both parts of the claim are present. ✓
  • "When ye plead for the wrecked and fallen, / The exile from far-distant shores, / Remember that men are still wasting / Life's crimson around your own doors." (from "An Appeal to My Countrywomen")": An appeal to advocate for the vulnerable — it urges action but does not describe mistreatment followed by divine punishment. ✗
  • "Say not the age is hard and cold-- / I think it brave and grand; / When men of diverse sects and creeds / Are clasping hand in hand." (from "The Present Age")": A hopeful statement about unity across differences. No mistreatment, no divine judgment. ✗
  • "God help our native land, / Bring surcease to her strife, / And shower from thy hand / A more abundant life." (from "God Bless Our Native Land")": A prayer for national blessing. It invokes God but not as judgment against oppressors — and no mistreatment is described. ✗
That matches the correct answer — A is correct.
